sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 58, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at row 25-27, col 16-19, above the left frame (row 28-31, col 16-19). Frame is still 'c'. Player blocked from moving DOWN through frame.
- Goal model: Collect all three frames. Right frame collected. Need to activate and collect left and center-below frames.
- Plan: Try pressing SPACE to see if it collects the frame even when it's 'c', or try a different approach.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 57 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the player's white head has moved to row 20-24, col 16-19 (above the green body). The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. The frame is still 'c'.
Wait, the white head moved UP. Let me check the segmentation to see what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Player position:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nFrames:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Compare with previous frame
if previous_frame:
    prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
    prev_nodes = prev_seg['nodes']
    print("\nPrevious player position:")
    for n in prev_nodes:
        if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
            print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    print("\nPrevious frames:")
    for n in prev_nodes:
        if n['color'] in ['c', 'G']:
            print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Pressing SPACE caused the white head to shrink from [[20, 16], [20, 19], [24, 19], [24, 16]] to [[24, 16], [24, 19]] (just 1 row). Also, a new 'G' frame appeared at [[20, 16], [20, 19], [23, 19], [23, 16]]!
So pressing SPACE when above the left frame created a 'G' frame above the player! The white head shrank and a 'G' frame appeared where the white head used to be.
This is different from the right frame collection. Let me think about this...
For the right frame:
- Player was below the frame (row 28-31, col 44-47)
- Frame was at row 24-27, col 44-47
- Pressing SPACE collected the frame, and the white head expanded to cover the frame's area
For the left frame:
- Player was above the frame (row 25-27, col 16-19)
- Frame was at row 28-31, col 16-19
- Pressing SPACE created a 'G' frame above the player (row 20-23, col 16-19)
- The white head shrank to just row 24
So it seems like pressing SPACE "pushes" the frame in the opposite direction. When the player is below the frame, pressing SPACE pulls the frame down (collects it). When the player is above the frame, pressing SPACE pushes the frame up.
Wait, but the 'G' frame is at row 20-23, which is above the player. The left frame is still at row 28-31, col 16-19, and it's still 'c'.
So pressing SPACE didn't collect the left frame; it created a new 'G' frame above the player.
Let me try pressing SPACE again to see if it does something else.
Actually, let me reconsider. The 'G' frame at row 20-23 might be the "activated" version of the left frame. The left frame at row 28-31 is still 'c', but there's a new 'G' frame above.
Maybe the goal is to collect the 'G' frames, not the 'c' frames. The right frame became 'G' when the player was below it, and then pressing SPACE collected it. For the left frame, pressing SPACE created a 'G' frame above the player.
Let me try pressing SPACE again to see if it collects the 'G' frame.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Pressing SPACE above the left frame created a 'G' frame at row 20-23, col 16-19. The white head shrank to row 24. The left frame at row 28-31 is still 'c'.
Goal model: Need to understand the 'G' frame mechanic. Maybe 'G' frames are collectible.
Plan: Try pressing SPACE again to see if it collects the 'G' frame.
